title: Named exports — share specific values
instruction:
Named exports let you export specific names from a module. Callers import only what they need.

Simulate a math utilities module with named exports.
successMessage: Named exports let callers `import { clamp, lerp } from './math'` — importing only what they need. Tree-shaking removes unused exports in production builds.

title: Type exports — share type definitions
instruction:
Type-only exports (`export type`) are erased at compile time — they produce no JavaScript output. Use `import type` to import them.

Create and use type-only exports.
successMessage: Type exports compile to nothing — they're purely for TypeScript's type checker. `import type { User }` makes this explicit and prevents accidentally importing types at runtime.

title: Re-exporting — barrel files
instruction:
A barrel file (`index.ts`) re-exports from multiple modules, giving callers a single clean import path.

Simulate a component library barrel file.
successMessage: Barrel files (`index.ts`) aggregate re-exports: `export { Button } from './Button'`. Callers import from one path instead of knowing the internal file structure. Standard in every React/Vue component library.

title: Namespace imports — import everything
instruction:
`import * as name` imports all named exports into a namespace object. Useful when a module has many related exports you want to group.

Use namespace import for validation utilities.
expectedOutput: password: Must be at least 8 characters
bio: Must be at most 10 characters
successMessage: Namespace imports let you call `validate.required()` instead of remembering individual function names. The namespace acts as documentation — it's clear these functions all relate to validation.

title: Dynamic imports — lazy loading
instruction:
Dynamic `import()` loads a module on demand instead of at startup. This reduces initial bundle size and enables code splitting.

Simulate lazy-loading a heavy utility.
successMessage: Dynamic imports return a Promise — they load asynchronously. In Next.js and similar frameworks, this enables automatic code splitting — the CSV formatter is only loaded when needed.

title: Module augmentation — extend third-party types
instruction:
Module augmentation lets you add to an existing module's types. This is how you add custom properties to Express's `Request` or extend global types.

Augment a module to add custom properties.
successMessage: Module augmentation adds types to existing modules without modifying their source. In real Express code: `declare module 'express' { interface Request { user?: AuthUser } }` — and every request handler gets the typed `user` property.

title: Declaration merging — extend interfaces
instruction:
TypeScript merges multiple interface declarations with the same name. This is how the DOM types are built — browser APIs add to existing interfaces.

Use declaration merging to extend a configuration interface.
successMessage: Declaration merging combines multiple interface declarations. The object must satisfy ALL merged declarations. This is how DOM libraries add properties to `Window`, `Element`, etc. across multiple `.d.ts` files.

title: ESM vs CommonJS — module systems
instruction:
TypeScript supports both ES Modules (import/export) and CommonJS (require/module.exports). Understanding the difference matters for Node.js compatibility.

See how module imports work in TypeScript.
successMessage: ES Modules (`import/export`) are the standard in TypeScript. The `tsconfig.json` `module` setting controls the output format — `commonjs` for Node.js, `esnext` for browsers and modern Node.js.

title: Circular dependencies — detecting and avoiding
instruction:
Circular imports occur when module A imports from module B, and module B imports from module A. TypeScript handles them but they can cause runtime issues.

See how to restructure code to avoid circular dependencies.
successMessage: Extracting shared types to a third module breaks the circular dependency. The dependency graph becomes a DAG (directed acyclic graph): app → services → shared-types. No cycles.

title: Path aliases — clean import paths
instruction:
TypeScript path aliases let you import with clean paths like `@/components/Button` instead of `../../../components/Button`. Configured in `tsconfig.json`.

See how path alias imports look in practice.
successMessage: Path aliases eliminate `../../../../` chains. Add to `tsconfig.json`: `{ "paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] } }`. Most Next.js projects configure `@/*` by default. Bundlers (webpack, Vite) must also be configured to resolve the aliases.

title: Conditional module loading — environment-specific code
instruction:
Sometimes you want different implementations for different environments (browser vs Node.js). TypeScript can handle this with conditional imports and type guards.

Load the right implementation based on the environment.
successMessage: This pattern — a factory that returns an implementation matching the environment — is used in libraries like Axios (browser/Node.js), uuid (crypto-based vs Math.random), and many cross-platform packages.

title: Strict mode — maximum type safety
instruction:
TypeScript's `strict` mode enables the strongest type checking. Understanding what it enables helps you write safer code from the start.

See the categories of checks that strict mode enables.
successMessage: Strict mode (`"strict": true` in tsconfig) enables: `strictNullChecks`, `strictFunctionTypes`, `noImplicitAny`, `strictPropertyInitialization`, and more. Every serious TypeScript project starts with `strict: true`.
